channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.gamedeveloper.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.gamedeveloper.com

item.title: Moss developer Polyarc is laying off staff
item.content:encoded:
Polyarc, the VR developer best known for the Moss series, is laying off an unspecified number of staff.

The company announced the job cuts yesterday via LinkedIn. Polyarc says the decision came as a result of being unable to secure funding after the cancellation of a major project. This led the developer to "significantly reduce the size of the company," albeit without sharing a specific number.

"We’re saying goodbye to many talented people who have been a meaningful part of what we’ve built," the announcement continues. "We want to thank everyone impacted. Your work, creativity, and care mattered. We made wonderful games together, and we would not have made it this far without you. Your contributions will continue to shape our culture and the games we make."

Polyarc claims it will be sharing a spreadsheet in the next few days with information about those impacted.

"It is a sad, sad day to say the least," wrote Polyarc's former engineering lead. "I have had the privilege of working with some of the best, most talented people across all disciplines. I've made friends and contributed to wonderful, amazing games. I will miss working with everyone."

Related:An Emmy-winning animation studio just bought the rights to multiple Roblox titles

"A job is a job but the people is what makes it and that is what is really eating me up," echoed Polyarc's former design director. "Polyarc housed the best people I have ever worked with in the industry, people who I was excited to work with and learn from everyday and now that has to pause for now."

It might only be Tuesday, but this is already a particularly brutal week with multiple layoffs and studio closures. So far, Series Entertainment-owned mobile studio Pixelberry reportedly cut jobs, while Embracer Group laid off 124 employees at Eidos Montreal. Then, both Ivy Road and Rec Room announced their closures.

Multiple other layoff rounds took place throughout March, extending throughout Sony, Ubisoft, EA, and Crystal Dynamics, as well as Epic Games, the latter of which laid off over 1,000 employees.

About the Author

Contributing Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Diego Nicolás Argüello is a freelance journalist and critic from Argentina. Video games helped him to learn English, so now he covers them for places like The New York Times, NPR, Rolling Stone, and more. He also runs Into the Spine, a site dedicated to fostering and supporting new writers, and co-hosted Turnabout Breakdown, a podcast about the Ace Attorney series. He’s most likely playing a rhythm game as you read this.



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/moss-developer-polyarc-is-laying-off-staff
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: An Emmy-winning animation studio just bought the rights to multiple Roblox titles
item.content:encoded:
Chris Kerr,

Senior Editor, News,GameDeveloper.com

March 31, 2026

1 Min Read

Baobab Studios has acquired the exclusive rights to Roblox titles Natural Disaster Survival, Barry’s Prison Run, and Deepwoken for an undisclosed fee. 

The Emmy Award-winning animation studio was co-founded by former Zynga vice president of games Maureen Fan, former Pixar technical director Larry Cutler, and Madagascar screenwriter and director Eric Darnell. 

Baobab is now actively developing animated feature-length movies and television series based on its slate of Roblox experiences, which it explained have collectively generated around 10 billion visits. 

Notably, Baobab CEO Maureen Fan said the company understands what drives success on Roblox and beyond, having previously created its own experiences on the platform. 

"We're not just looking at scale—we're looking at what sustains," she explained. "Having built our own Roblox games, we sought titles that combine reach with retention, deepen engagement, and clear world-building that can carry powerfully into film and television."

Related:Rec Room shutting down after failing to become 'sustainably profitable'

Discussing the company's interest in Roblox, Baobab co-founder Eric Darnell said the user-generated content (UGC) platform has established itself as an entertainment juggernaut.

"These games have fans who are genuinely invested in these worlds, and that level of engagement is the foundation we’ll build on to welcome new audiences while preserving the passion that already exists," he added. 

Roblox is indeed rather popular. The platform boasted 144 million daily active users and delivered 35 billion hours of engagement during the quarter ended December 30, 2025.

It has, however, also been widely criticised (and litigated against) for allegedly failing to protect young users from child predators and other bad actors. Roblox has continued to implement new safety tools and parental controls in an effort to address those concerns. 

About the Author

Senior Editor, News, GameDeveloper.com

Game Developer news editor Chris Kerr is an award-winning reporter with over a decade of experience in the game industry. His byline has appeared in notable print and digital publications including Edge, Stuff, Wireframe, International Business Times, and PocketGamer.biz. Throughout his career, Chris has covered major industry events including GDC, PAX Australia, Gamescom, Paris Games Week, and Develop Brighton. 



https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/an-emmy-winning-animation-studio-just-bought-the-rights-to-multiple-roblox-titles
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
